data.title: SK Cross-Market Arbitrage: A Frenzy of Arbitrage Across US Stocks, Korean Stocks, and Crypto Markets
data.description:
Author: Jae, PANews

As the demand for memory chips from major US AI companies continues to grow, semiconductor giant SK Hynix has become the focus of global capital, taking advantage of the situation to list on Nasdaq and issue ADRs (American Depositary Receipts) to further broaden its financing channels.

Just two weeks after its listing, SK Hynix's US ADRs (stock code SKHY) have maintained an average premium of around 30% relative to its domestic South Korean ordinary shares, exhibiting a rare phenomenon of "different prices for the same stock."

The emergence of high premiums has also made cross-market arbitrage of SK Hynix a "gold mine," with an arbitrage game reaching its climax last week, spanning from South Korean spot stocks and US ADRs to the crypto market. This article will analyze five arbitrage strategies operating across multiple markets, including US stocks, South Korean stocks, crypto exchanges, and DeFi.

The triple "fault" fuels arbitrage premiums. The surge in cross-market arbitrage for SK Hynix is ​​essentially the result of a combination of factors: blocked two-way conversion channels, spillover of sentiment from the crypto market, and misaligned trading time zones. The structural primary reason for the traditional market premium lies in the lack of interconnectivity between US ADRs and Korean spot ADRs. On July 9th, SK Hynix issued 177.9 million ADRs at $149 per share, raising $26.5 billion, making it the largest ADR issuance in the history of a foreign company. The issuance was oversubscribed by more than 7 times, with top global institutions such as Baillie Gifford and Coatue securing a total of $5 billion in cornerstone shares. However, the issued shares only represent approximately 2.5% of the company's total share capital, making the circulating shares inherently scarce. In principle, every 10 SKHY shares correspond to 1 Korean stock. However, due to the initial closed-loop conversion channel, the two cannot be freely exchanged. In a normal ADR mechanism, arbitrageurs can buy low-priced domestic common stock, convert it to ADRs, and then sell it at a higher price in the US market, ultimately eliminating the price difference. However, SKHY's ADRs are not created by depositing existing shares, but by issuing new shares. The Korea Depository & Clearing Corporation (KDS) stipulates that applications for two-way conversion between SKHY common stock and ADRs cannot be initiated until July 29th. Before then, the market only supports the one-way operation of canceling ADRs to exchange for Korean stocks; the reverse ADR supply channel has not yet been opened. On this single-track path, the huge demand for US stock funds has squeezed into the scarce ADR circulating supply, coupled with the market heat of the AI ​​storage sector, continuously pushing up the SKHY premium. This is similar to the long-standing premium of TSMC ADRs, only with Hynix's volatility being more pronounced. While the market expects the premium to narrow after the two-way conversion channel opens at the end of July following the release of SEC F-6 and other regulatory documents, the significant price difference still creates excellent opportunities for arbitrageurs until then. The root cause of the premium in the crypto market is the overflow of "bottom-fishing" sentiment. Since many players cannot directly open accounts with Korean brokers to trade Korean stocks, they can only turn their attention to Hynix's perpetual contracts listed on crypto exchanges. During last week's sharp correction, a large number of retail investors flocked to Binance and Hyperliquid to "bottom-fish" with perpetual contracts, causing the platforms' funding rates to soar. Over the past 30 days, the annualized funding rate (APR) of Hynix perpetual contracts on cryptocurrency trading platforms has mostly remained above 30%. Furthermore, time zone discrepancies have led to temporary pricing misalignments. During the closure of the Korean and US stock markets, perpetual contracts in the cryptocurrency market lack external price anchors and rely primarily on index-weighted moving averages (EWMA) for pricing. This can cause price "front-running" or lag, creating arbitrage opportunities. Arbitrage Strategy Diversification: From "Arbitrage" to "Fixed Income" Significant pricing discrepancies in the same underlying asset across different markets have created abundant arbitrage opportunities. As participants continue to enter the market, arbitrage strategies have rapidly evolved from the most basic "cross-market arbitrage" to complex combinations incorporating cryptocurrency tools. Strategy 1: Cross-Market Premium Convergence Game The most classic strategy is to "buy Korean spot stocks + short US ADRs," betting on future price convergence. Assuming a premium of over 35% between US ADRs and Korean spot stocks, users can buy Korean spot stocks through Interactive Brokers (IBKR) while simultaneously shorting SKHY. Arbitrage trader yourQuantGuy points out that the main cost of this strategy is short selling interest: SKHY's annualized short selling interest initially reached as high as 50%, but quickly declined to the 2%-5% range as supply increased. Combined with the fact that the combined margin account's long and short positions barely tie up capital, the holding cost will be significantly reduced. Closing the position in batches when the premium falls below 30% can achieve a return of over 4%. It's important to be wary that this isn't risk-free arbitrage, but rather a risk-reward trade based on "betting on premium convergence." Arbitrageurs generally assume the premium between Korean spot stocks and US ADRs will revert to its previous level, but referring to TSMC's precedent: even with the opening of swap channels, ADR premiums may become the norm. Hynix ADRs also have an issuance cap, and retail conversion has procedural hurdles. If the supply release after the two-way conversion begins falls short of expectations, the premium may become a long-term phenomenon, and positions betting on convergence will face continuous floating losses. Strategy Two: Cash & Futures Arbitrage + Fee Harvesting With major crypto trading platforms successively listing Hynix perpetual contracts, on-chain trading has become one of the most active battlegrounds for arbitrage. Buy Korean stock spot through a brokerage firm, and simultaneously short a perpetual contract of equal value on Binance or Hyperliquid. The spot long position and the contract short position hedge against stock price volatility risk, but the short position will receive high funding fees paid by the long position. Strategy Three: Arbitrage through Differences in Trading Platform Rules + "EWMA Mechanism" Due to differences in index compilation rules and funding fee settlement mechanisms among major cryptocurrency trading platforms, price and fee differences generally exist between platforms. Index calculation rules during non-trading hours: Binance uses the EWMA index to calculate the mark price, making it easy for contracts to "rush" to profit from price differences before the market opens; Hyperliquid references pre-market and post-market auction prices; OKX uses a proportional combination of the indices of the former two as its pricing benchmark. Arbitrage trader Sanfen stated that rule differences have led to the norm of "Binance perpetual contract price > OKX > Hyperliquid". A loophole in the Cap mechanism exists: Binance's single-transaction funding rate cap (Cap) was once locked at 0.5% (8 hours), while Hyperliquid settles hourly with no cap, causing the Hynix contract price spread to widen to $30. "Smart money" traders made hundreds of thousands of dollars in a short period by "shorting Binance + going long Hyperliquid". However, the funding rules, index components, and settlement frequencies of crypto trading platforms are not fixed. In mid-July, Binance changed the Hynix perpetual contract funding rate from 8-hour settlement to 4-hour settlement, causing the price spread to narrow by nearly half within half a day. Arbitrage strategies relying on stable mechanisms need to constantly pay attention to rule changes. Strategy 4: Cross-Market Derivatives + ETF Arbitrage Last Friday (July 17th), during the period when the Korean stock market was closed and the Hong Kong stock market was trading normally, the Hong Kong stock 2x long Hynix ETF experienced a discount of over 20% due to panic selling. Traders could hedge their exposure by buying the discounted ETF and shorting the on-chain perpetual contract, and then close their positions after the discount narrowed when the Korean stock market opened. Strategy 5: Interest Rate Derivatives to Lock in "Delta-Neutral Fixed Income" On July 20th, Pendle's structured interest rate platform Boros launched the funding rate market for Hyperliquid Hynix perpetual contracts, allowing users to convert their previously floating funding rates into fixed rates, completing a key link in the arbitrage ecosystem. The market has quickly developed Delta-neutral fixed-income strategies: 1. Spot Leg: Buy Hynix Korean stock on traditional brokerages like IBKR; 2. Contract Leg: Open an equivalent value of short Hynix perpetual contracts on Hyperliquid, collecting floating funding rates; 3. Fixed Leg: Short YU on Boros (i.e., sell floating rates), locking in approximately 30% fixed income. The spot and perpetual contracts achieve Delta neutrality, with the two floating rate legs offsetting each other, resulting in a net profit from the fixed rates on the Boros side. However, the liquidity of SK Hynix-related assets, whether in Boros' interest rate market or Hyperliquid's order book, remains somewhat weaker compared to traditional stock markets. Large positions can easily lead to significant price differences and slippage losses when entering and exiting trades, lowering the overall net return of the strategy. Additionally, cross-border transaction costs are worth noting. Hidden costs such as fluctuations in the Korean won exchange rate, changes in securities lending interest rates, and the efficiency of cross-border settlement can continuously erode profits, making it difficult for ordinary investors to cover all risks. Even so, as long as fees and price differences exist, arbitrageurs will not leave the market. The cross-market arbitrage frenzy surrounding SK Hynix is ​​also a microcosm of the further intertwining of traditional financial assets and DeFi infrastructure. Institutional barriers in traditional markets are rapidly broken down on the blockchain into multiple layers of trading tools, such as spot price spreads, cross-platform fee spreads, and interest rate derivatives, creating a richer trading dimension and higher capital efficiency than traditional markets. At the end of July, with the approach of Hynix's ADR swap window and earnings report date, the convergence of these two events may become a key turning point in the premium trend. This arbitrage game spanning multiple markets may present another fascinating picture.

data.title: When ETH is deeply embedded in the balance sheet: Where is staking headed next?
data.description:
Author: imToken

Currently, if you want to become an Ethereum validator directly, you have to wait in line for over a month.

As of July 22, there were still approximately 2.5 million ETH in the Ethereum staking queue, with an estimated waiting time of over 43 days. In contrast, the waiting time to exit the queue is only a few minutes, almost negligible.

From the numbers alone, there is no doubt that the increasing amount of ETH staking is siphoning off the market's circulating supply.

But what's more noteworthy than the growth in staking volume is that the queuing process is beginning to become a matter of capital efficiency. After all, for ETH treasury companies and institutions that choose the native staking path, a queuing period of over 40 days means that a considerable amount of assets cannot generate staking rewards temporarily, and asset allocation, liquidity arrangements, and opportunity costs all need to be recalculated.

Ultimately, as ETH is more deeply integrated into the balance sheet, the problem facing staking is no longer just "how to get more people to participate," but is beginning to become a more traditional and complex asset management issue.

I. Record High Staking Ratio: How to Understand the Queuing?

Ethereum's current high staking ratio did not suddenly appear at a certain point in time.

In 2023, Shapela (Shanghai + Capella) upgraded and opened the staking withdrawal function, allowing validators to retrieve their staked principal and rewards at the protocol level. This created a relatively complete entry, operation, and exit loop for ETH staking. Subsequently, the derivatives market based on LSD expanded rapidly, driving the ETH staking ratio higher and higher.

As of the time of writing, the amount of ETH staked has exceeded 40 million, worth approximately $140 billion at the current price, accounting for more than 33% of the total supply. This is a significant increase compared to the staking ratio of about 10% a few years ago, setting a new historical high.

In other words, currently, more than 1 out of every 3 ETH is being staked. With the staking rate reaching new highs, the persistently high entry queue has also exposed a new problem. As is well known, Ethereum's entry and exit queues are essentially a rate-limiting mechanism to protect consensus stability. New ETH cannot enter the validator set indefinitely, nor can exits occur in a concentrated manner within a short period. The protocol sets the number of ETH that can be processed per Epoch based on the current validator size. When the funds applying to enter or exit exceed the processing capacity, a queue will form. From this perspective, the 2.5 million ETH queuing to enter firstly indicates that the market demand for staking capacity far exceeds the protocol's current release rate—this could include newly entering long-term funds, treasury companies deploying existing holdings, staking service providers adjusting their validator structure, and institutions transferring ETH from their escrow accounts to the staking system. Therefore, this actually sends a clear signal: at least at the current stage, the funds willing to let ETH enter the staking system far exceed the funds actively withdrawing from the validator set. This is significantly different from the staking logic at the initial launch of Beacon Chain. Early ETH staking was more like a network participation mechanism for technical users, independent validators, and long-term Ethereum supporters. Participants ran nodes, maintained the network, and assumed technical risks in exchange for protocol rewards. With the rise of liquidity staking, it gradually became a product for ordinary token holders to obtain on-chain rewards. For example, exchange staking, staking as a service, and staking pools lowered the technical barrier. Liquidity staking protocols such as Lido and Rocket Pool further increased the availability of staking funds, allowing users to obtain liquidity staking tokens such as stETH and rETH after staking ETH. These tokens can be transferred and traded, and can also be used in lending, liquidity pools, and other DeFi protocols. Today, with a large amount of ETH entering corporate treasuries, fund products, and professional custody systems, staking is undoubtedly moving towards its third stage, shifting from "who can participate in staking" to "how large-scale ETH should be managed." Of course, the institutionalization mentioned here does not mean that early staking was entirely dominated by retail investors, nor does it mean that institutions will replace ordinary users. More accurately, the focus of market discussion is changing: In the past, the focus was on how ordinary users could obtain staking rewards. Now, the focus is on how staking can become a standardized financial management capability once hundreds of thousands or even millions of ETH enter a company's balance sheet.

II. Structural Changes Behind Institutions Like BitMine

The emergence of ETH Treasury companies is making this change even more apparent.

Because the core logic of Bitcoin Treasury companies is to continuously accumulate BTC through financing and capital market operations, increasing the number of Bitcoins corresponding to each share; but for ETH Treasury companies, holding assets is not the end of the strategy.

This is because the core logic of Bitcoin Treasury companies is to continuously accumulate BTC through financing and capital market operations, increasing the number of Bitcoins corresponding to each share; but for ETH Treasury companies, holding assets is not the end of the strategy.

After all, BTC itself doesn't offer native staking rewards. Holders typically need to introduce lending, custody, derivatives, or other counterparty risks to obtain additional returns. ETH, on the other hand, can directly participate in Ethereum consensus, earning protocol rewards without selling assets. This naturally adds an extra layer of operational space to the ETH treasury; besides deciding how much ETH to buy, it also needs to decide how that ETH will be deployed. BitMine's actions are a prime example of this institutional approach. According to its latest disclosed data, as of July 19, BitMine held a total of 5,777,468 ETH, accounting for approximately 4.8% of the total ETH supply. Of this, 4.917 million ETH were staked, representing 85% of its total ETH holdings, valued at approximately $9.2 billion. Based on the ETH price at the time and BitMine's own 2.67% seven-day annualized staking yield, the company expected to earn approximately $247 million in staking income annually; if all of its ETH were eventually staked, the annualized reward could reach approximately $290 million. More noteworthy is the speed at which this figure is changing. In early February of this year, BitMine had approximately 2.8975 million ETH staked, representing about 67% of its holdings at the time. By mid-July, this had increased to approximately 4.9172 million ETH. In other words, in less than six months, BitMine deployed over 2 million new ETH, raising its staking coverage ratio from approximately two-thirds to 85%. This demonstrates that Tom Lee and BitMine are visibly staking their ETH holdings, transforming it from a mere crypto asset waiting for price appreciation into a fundamental on-chain asset with inherent yield potential. For ordinary investors, the staking ratio might simply be a yield option, but for BitMine, it is becoming a key treasury performance indicator alongside ETH holdings, net asset value per share, and financing costs. Meanwhile, BitMine also launched its own institutional-grade staking platform, MAVAN, to serve its own ETH treasury. It also plans to provide staking infrastructure to institutional investors, custodians, and ecosystem partners in the future. This means that staking serves at least three roles for BitMine: First, it adds an ETH-based return layer to long-term holdings; second, staking rewards can be reinvested, increasing the amount of ETH held in the treasury; and finally, when its self-built validator capabilities are opened to the outside world, the staking infrastructure itself may become a service business. SharpLink further extends this logic from native staking to active yield management. For it, basic staking returns are just the starting point; some of the staked ETH can continue to enter the on-chain yield fund and be allocated to DeFi strategies such as liquidity and lending. The changes in Lido V3 occur at the infrastructure layer. Previously, users and institutions primarily entered unified liquidity staking pools. Now, institutions can use more independent staking vaults, choosing their own node operators, fee structures, and risk parameters, while retaining the option to obtain stETH liquidity. This means that liquidity staking is moving from a standardized product towards an isolated, customizable institutional-grade infrastructure. Therefore, the competition among ETH treasury companies may not only be about who holds more, but also about who can manage these ETH at lower costs, with higher uptime, and more robust risk control. From this perspective, ETH is also transforming from a crypto asset waiting for price increases into an asset requiring continuous operation. III. Why is Staking More Important When the Yield Isn't High? As of the time of writing, the Ethereum network's staking APR is approximately 2.64%. Realistically speaking, compared to some DeFi products, this level is not particularly outstanding, and as the amount of ETH staked continues to increase, this basic yield may be further diluted. However, institutional demand for staking cannot be understood solely in terms of yield—staking reduces their opportunity cost of holding ETH long-term. For short-term investors, an annualized return of 2% to 3% is unlikely to offset the price volatility of ETH itself. But for treasury companies, funds, or large addresses that have decided to hold ETH long-term, the issue is that since ETH is already on their balance sheets, they must continuously acquire more ETH through network security participation without giving up their ETH price exposure. This is easy to understand. For an ordinary user holding 100 ETH, a 2.6% return might not be significant; however, for a treasury firm holding millions of ETH, the same return would generate substantial absolute income, gradually influencing the number of ETH per share through long-term reinvestment. This is also one of the key differences between ETH and BTC in their treasury narratives. Therefore, when ETH enters an institution's balance sheet, the treasury department is not dealing with a static position, but rather an on-chain asset that can be continuously deployed, accounted for, and adjusted. Furthermore, as institutional participation increases, the native staking yield may also assume another function: becoming the yield benchmark for the entire ETH asset system. This is easy to understand. For example, in the future, when a DeFi strategy promises returns of 5%, 8%, or even higher, institutions won't be comparing "having a return" or "not having a return," but rather how much more it earns relative to the native staking return of approximately 2.6%, and what additional risks it takes on. Lending, liquidity making, structured products, and restaking strategies all need to demonstrate the reasonableness of their risk-reward ratio based on this fundamental return. From this perspective, the importance of the next stage of staking lies not only in how much ETH it brings to token holders, but also in its becoming the underlying benchmark for measuring other on-chain strategies. However, it cannot be simply regarded as Ethereum's "risk-free rate." Stakers need to bear risks such as ETH price fluctuations, validator offline events, node failures, and potential penalties. Participation through service providers will also increase the risks for operators and custodians. If it continues to enter DeFi, the risks will be compounded with the increase in the number of protocol and strategy layers. In addition, higher staking ratios do not only have positive effects. If new funds are mainly concentrated in the hands of a few treasury companies, custodians, liquidity staking protocols, and node operators, it is likely to exacerbate the centralization of validators, cloud service providers, and jurisdictions. Therefore, as staking evolves from a network participation mechanism into an institutional asset allocation tool, Ethereum needs to address not only how to absorb more funds, but also how to maintain a balance between capital efficiency, institutional demand, and decentralization. In conclusion, from the initial 32 ETH requirement for validator nodes at the launch of Beacon Chain, to the liquidity staking protocol lowering the participation threshold, and now to treasury companies, self-built validator networks, and institutional on-chain yield funds, the changes in staking essentially reflect the market's changing understanding of ETH. It initially served as a mechanism for participating in network consensus, then became a tool for ordinary users to obtain on-chain rewards, and is now entering company balance sheets, custody systems, and professional yield management frameworks. For these long-term holders, a 2% to 3% return may not be impressive. However, as long as ETH is no longer confined to addresses or custodian accounts waiting for its price to rise, but can participate in cybersecurity, earn protocol rewards, and continuously reinvest, while maintaining a certain level of liquidity, it will help accelerate its transformation into an underlying asset for other financial strategies. This is also the new era's challenge for ETH.

data.title: A massive $13.58 million long position in SPCX and the SEC is propelling US stocks toward all-day trading.
data.description:
Headline

▌A whale heavily invested $13.58 million in SPCX, with an average entry price of $126.04

On July 23, according to Onchain Lens monitoring, Hyperliquid whale 0x3527…1e16 held a long position of 122,000 SPCX, worth approximately $13.58 million. The current average entry price was $126.04, while the market price has fallen to $111.29, resulting in a current unrealized loss of approximately $1.8 million. The liquidation price is $22.27.

▌US SEC Pushes for 24-Hour Trading Roundtable to Hold 24-Hour Trading

On July 23, the US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) announced that it will hold a roundtable meeting at its Washington headquarters on September 17, 2026, to discuss issues related to the transition of the US stock market to 24-hour trading, including preparations to support overnight trading, operations and resilience in a 24-hour market, and the opportunities and challenges of expanding trading hours. SEC Chairman Paul Atkins stated in a press release, "We are moving towards a new day—and a new night—for the US stock market. With the expansion to overnight trading, I am excited to see the US stock market align with those markets that have already achieved continuous trading, and I look forward to achieving a balance between 24-hour trading and the crucial protection of investors and clients."

Policy

▌UK HMRC Discloses Crypto Tax Recovery Results: Over £8 Million Recovered in Two Years

On July 24, the UK's HMRC disclosed that since launching a special campaign for cryptocurrency tax compliance in November 2023, it has reached disclosed settlements with 502 cryptocurrency investors over the past two years, recovering over £8 million in taxes, with an average settlement of approximately £16,600 per settlement. In the 2024/25 fiscal year, 280 settlements were reached, involving £3.54 million; in the 2025/26 fiscal year, 222 settlements were reached, with the amount rising to approximately £4.78 million.

▌Iraqi Prime Minister: Iraq will not allow actions threatening Iran to be launched from its territory

On July 24, according to a statement released by the Iraqi Prime Minister's press office on the 23rd, Iraqi Prime Minister Zaidi visited Iran that day and held talks with Iranian President Pezechzian in Tehran, the Iranian capital.

Zaidi stated that Iraq and Iran's security are closely intertwined, and Iraq will never allow any actions threatening Iran to originate from its territory. Pezechian said that security and stability are of great importance to the development of bilateral relations. (CCTV)

▌South Korea Raises Cash Deposit Requirements for Single-Stock Leveraged ETF Trading Three Days Ahead of Schedule

On July 24, according to Yonhap News Agency, in response to continuous criticism that the government's implementation of regulations on single-stock leveraged investment was too late, the South Korean government decided to advance the strengthening of basic margin requirements, originally scheduled for next month, to July 31, three days earlier than originally planned. The Financial Services Commission (FSC) of South Korea stated on the 24th that, in order to quickly stabilize market demand, the strengthening of basic margin requirements, originally scheduled for August, will be implemented on the 31st.

... On July 16, the South Korean Financial Services Commission announced a supplementary plan, the core of which includes raising the basic margin requirement from the current 10 million won to 30 million won, and requiring it to be in cash only. (Jinshi)

Blockchain and AI Applications

▌Black Forest Labs Launches Robot Model, Entering the Field of Physical AI

On July 24, the German artificial intelligence research laboratory Black Forest Labs was testing its first model that can be used for robotics (AI), expanding its business from photorealistic image generation to the field of physical AI. The company's Flux 3 model can learn from images, videos, and audio, and can predict the next action based on what it sees, enabling it to compete in the field of "visual intelligence". Black Forest Labs has partnered with Mimic Robotics AG to develop a motion model called Flux-mimic and is testing it with manufacturing partners including Audi AG.

On July 24th, cryptocurrency payment platform MoonPay announced that it now supports US users using Discover Network cards for cryptocurrency transactions, becoming the third largest US card network to be integrated after Visa and Mastercard. The company stated that this move aims to expand payment compatibility and reduce checkout barriers. Currently, MoonPay supports various payment methods including Apple Pay, Google Pay, PayPal, Venmo, and bank transfers. However, whether a specific card can be used for cryptocurrency transactions still depends on the issuing bank's policy. MoonPay claims to have over 30 million users worldwide and more than 500 corporate clients.

On July 24, cryptocurrency exchange Gemini donated approximately $10 million worth of Bitcoin to MAGA Inc., a super PAC supporting US President Donald Trump. According to a report filed with the Federal Election Commission (FEC), Gemini co-founders Cameron Winklevoss and Tyler Winklevoss made the donation in two separate transactions on June 19, each exceeding $5 million. This donation comes approximately three weeks after the CFTC and Gemini jointly filed a motion in the Southern District of New York federal court seeking to overturn a $5 million settlement reached between them in January 2025. The settlement stemmed from the CFTC's previous allegations that Gemini made false or misleading statements. MAGA Inc. can use the funds to support Trump's independent political spending. Previously, the Winklevoss brothers each donated $1 million to Trump's 2024 campaign and $21 million worth of Bitcoin to the Digital Freedom Fund PAC to support the Trump administration's crypto policy direction. Currently, the court has not yet ruled on the CFTC's motion to withdraw its settlement with Gemini. Meanwhile, CFTC Chairman Michael Selig remains the agency's sole commissioner. As of June 30, MAGA Inc. had reported receiving over $397 million in funding. Alphabet's stake in Anthropic surged to approximately $124 billion on July 24, making it one of the company's most successful investments in history. **Important Economic News** Intel Corporation reported second-quarter revenue of $16.13 billion, a 25% year-over-year increase, on July 24th; the estimated revenue was $14.43 billion. Data center and artificial intelligence (AI) revenue reached $6.26 billion, exceeding analysts' expectations of $5.54 billion. Adjusted earnings per share (EPS) for the second quarter were $0.42, compared to analysts' expectations of $0.21. The company projects third-quarter revenue of $15.8-16.8 billion, compared to analysts' expectations of $15.06 billion. The company projects third-quarter adjusted EPS of $0.38, compared to analysts' expectations of $0.27.

▌The probability of the Federal Reserve keeping interest rates unchanged in July is 65.3%

According to CME's "FedWatch" on July 24: The probability of the Federal Reserve keeping interest rates unchanged in July is 65.3%, and the probability of a cumulative rate hike of 25 basis points is 34.7%. The probability of the Federal Reserve keeping interest rates unchanged by September is 17.6%, the probability of a cumulative rate hike of 25 basis points is 57%, and the probability of a cumulative rate hike of 50 basis points is 25.4%.

▌The US dollar index rose 0.32% on the 23rd

The US dollar index rose 0.32% on the 23rd, closing at 101.445 in the late trading session.

▌US military claims 13th consecutive night of strikes against Iran

July 24, US Central Command: The US military launched another nighttime strike against Iranian military targets at 6:45 p.m. Eastern Time today. This is the 13th consecutive night of such strikes, aimed at holding Iran accountable and weakening the threat posed by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps to commercial shipping. (Jin Shi)

▌US media: Iran rejects Trump's ceasefire proposal on Thursday

July 24, according to the New York Times: Iranian and Iraqi officials revealed that Iran rejected a ceasefire proposal put forward by US President Trump on Thursday, which was conveyed to Tehran by the Iraqi Prime Minister. This mediation effort ultimately failed, while Trump threatened to escalate the war and considered taking action against key Iranian infrastructure.

... (Golden Encyclopedia) Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" During Geopolitical Crises? Every geopolitical crisis causes gold prices to rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been substantiated by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits asymmetry: it falls with stocks but doesn't rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an overabundance of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "useful asset in times of crisis," and it can indeed function when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread application of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Google's stock price plummeted. Is Gemini dead?
data.description:
This week, Google, the former Silicon Valley giant, experienced its darkest moment in its AGI transformation: its stock price plummeted to a record low in a single day, wiping out billions of dollars in market value within hours. The market has directly targeted Google's core AI product—the Gemini series of models. The Gemini 3.5 Pro, originally scheduled for release at the end of June, has been delayed for several months due to the need for "significant performance improvements" and has yet to be officially unveiled. Is Google AI truly "dead"? Poor model performance, massive talent drain, and uncertainty about its overall direction… these questions are growing louder. Today, we'll take a deep dive into this issue.

 The Slap in the Face of Data: Gemini is Becoming a "Second-Rate Asset"

In the AI ​​industry, appearances are given by public relations, but substance comes from data.

In the AI ​​industry, appearances are given by public relations, but substance comes from data.

Even though Google launched Gemini 3.6 Flash yesterday at breakneck speed, attempting to stabilize its position through frequent iterations, the latest LMSYS Chatbot Arena (Global Large Model Arena) ranking competition has revealed the harsh reality. In the hardcore "coding" and "complex logic reasoning" arenas, while Gemini 3.6 Flash offers rapid speed improvements, its deep understanding capabilities still cannot rival GPT-5.6 Sol. The dominant position of the Claude 5 Fable. Even when facing direct competition from domestic models like the Kimi K3 and Qwen 3.8, its cost-performance advantage becomes increasingly apparent. The Great Talent Exodus: DeepMind's Internal "Silicon-Based Civil War" More fatal than the generational gap in models is the blood loss of the organization's soul. According to the latest report from The Information, DeepMind's internal engineering team has experienced its highest turnover rate since the AGI wave in the past three months. The geniuses who once defined the Transformer architecture are flocking to Anthropic, xAI, and even the newly established AI lab. During the week of June 18-25, 2026, at least five prominent researchers from Google DeepMind have confirmed or been reported to have left the company, with four moving to Anthropic and one to OpenAI. Their reasons for leaving were strikingly consistent: "Google's lengthy bureaucratic review process and extremely conservative alignment policies are stifling the original evolutionary potential of AI." To avoid the risks associated with so-called "political correctness," the Gemini series was forced to be subject to airtight underlying constraints. The direct consequence of this over-intervention is "model deintelligence"—when users need a sharp logical judgment, Gemini often provides a bunch of dogmatic, uncreative nonsense. The fatal flaw: The strongest AI genes, yet it lost the product war. The logic behind Google's failure is perplexing yet extremely cruel: it invented the Transformer, it possessed the most TPU computing power, and it even had a genius mind like DeepMind. However, in the AGI era, leading in scientific research does not equate to product success. The disconnect between "laboratory" and "assembly line": Google's organizational DNA is more like a university, tending to pursue perfect academic papers rather than minimalist product experiences. This DNA makes its engineering implementation extremely slow. While OpenAI had already captured users' minds through high-frequency iterations, Google was still internally debating a certain security metric for Gemini 3.6 Flash. The Ultimate Manifestation of the "Innovator's Dilemma": To preserve its annual profits of hundreds of billions of dollars from traditional search advertising, Google's path to productizing AI has been fraught with difficulty. This vacillation—wanting to retain the old world (the search ecosystem) while simultaneously striving to capture the new world (AGI)—often leaves its AI products as awkward, unfinished products. The Collapse of Engineering Efficiency: Despite possessing the world's strongest data and computing power, Google's product delivery cycle is measured in "quarters," while competitors such as xAI, Anthropic, and even its Chinese lab with only 1/10 of Google's computing power, are conducting model distillation and product optimization in "weeks" or even "days." This bloated nature of large companies has shown a fatal rejection reaction in the AI ​​battlefield, which demands rapid evolution.

 Final Judgment: When the Right to Define Shifts

Google's setback reveals a cruel truth to all AI practitioners:In AI In this era, those who hold the original definition of technology are not necessarily the ones who reap the benefits of the final product. The release of Flash 3.6 seems more like a defensive response from a giant facing a predator's onslaught, rather than a proactive restructuring of the future. As the "black box" of technology becomes increasingly transparent, engineering-driven responsiveness, product mindshare, and precise targeting of user anxieties are replacing the number of published papers as the new core competitive advantage.
